primarySources.title: The New Testament
primarySources.author: Various Authors
primarySources.text: The primary source for the life, teachings, death, and resurrection of Jesus Christ. Includes the Gospels (Matthew, Mark, Luke, and John), the Acts of the Apostles, the Epistles, and Revelation.

primarySources.title: The Old Testament
primarySources.author: Various Authors
primarySources.text: Considered by Christians to contain prophecies and prefigurations of Jesus Christ. Key passages include Isaiah 53 (the Suffering Servant), Psalm 22 (the cry of dereliction), and Micah 5:2 (birthplace in Bethlehem).

primarySources.title: Josephus, Antiquities of the Jews
primarySources.author: Flavius Josephus
primarySources.text: A first-century Jewish historian who mentions Jesus in his writings. The authenticity of the 'Testimonium Flavianum' is debated, but it provides evidence of Jesus' existence and the early Christian movement.

primarySources.title: The Gospel of Thomas
primarySources.author: Unknown
primarySources.text: A non-canonical gospel containing a collection of sayings attributed to Jesus, offering an alternative perspective on his teachings.

title: Christian - Jesus Christ
name: Jesus Christ
description:
Jesus Christ, also known as Yeshua Ha-Mashiach in Hebrew and often referred to as Jesus of Nazareth, is the central figure in Christianity. His origin story, as recounted in the New Testament Gospels, begins with a miraculous conception. Mary, a virgin betrothed to Joseph, was visited by the angel Gabriel, who announced that she would conceive and bear a son through the Holy Spirit (Luke 1:26-38). This virgin birth is a cornerstone of Christian theology, signifying Jesus' unique status as both fully human and fully divine. The Gospels of Matthew and Luke provide genealogies tracing Jesus' lineage back to King David, fulfilling Jewish messianic prophecies. His birth in Bethlehem, a small town in Judea, is another key element in these prophecies (Micah 5:2; Matthew 2:1-6). The circumstances of his birth, including the visit of the Magi guided by a star and the subsequent flight to Egypt to escape King Herod's infanticide, are rich in symbolic meaning, representing the recognition of Jesus' divinity by both Jews and Gentiles and the challenges he would face throughout his life.

Jesus' public ministry began around the age of thirty, after being baptized by John the Baptist in the Jordan River. This event marked the beginning of his messianic mission, as the Holy Spirit descended upon him in the form of a dove, and a voice from heaven proclaimed, "You are my beloved Son; with you I am well pleased" (Mark 1:9-11). For approximately three years, Jesus traveled throughout Galilee and Judea, preaching the kingdom of God, teaching in synagogues, and performing miracles. These miracles, which included healing the sick, casting out demons, raising the dead, and controlling nature, served as signs of his divine power and authority (Matthew 8-9; Mark 4-5; Luke 7-8; John 2, 11). His teachings, often delivered in parables, emphasized love, compassion, forgiveness, and the importance of inner transformation. The Sermon on the Mount (Matthew 5-7), a collection of Jesus' ethical teachings, is a central text in Christian morality, outlining principles such as the Beatitudes, the Golden Rule, and the call to love one's enemies.

Key myths and stories surrounding Jesus include the Last Supper, the Garden of Gethsemane, the trial before Pontius Pilate, the crucifixion, and the resurrection. The Last Supper, the final meal Jesus shared with his disciples, is commemorated by Christians as the institution of the Eucharist, or Holy Communion (Matthew 26:26-29; Mark 14:22-25; Luke 22:14-20; 1 Corinthians 11:23-26). In the Garden of Gethsemane, Jesus prayed in agony, anticipating his impending suffering and death (Matthew 26:36-46; Mark 14:32-42; Luke 22:39-46). His arrest, trial, and crucifixion, often referred to as the Passion, are central to Christian theology, representing the ultimate sacrifice for the sins of humanity. According to the Gospels, Jesus was crucified on a cross, died, and was buried in a tomb. On the third day, he rose from the dead, appearing to his disciples and others before ascending to heaven (Matthew 28; Mark 16; Luke 24; John 20-21; 1 Corinthians 15:3-8). The resurrection is the cornerstone of Christian faith, signifying victory over death and the promise of eternal life for believers.

The symbolism associated with Jesus is rich and multifaceted. He is often symbolized by the Lamb, representing his sacrificial death; the Cross, representing his crucifixion and redemption; and the Alpha and Omega, representing his eternal nature. The concept of the Trinity, the belief that God is one being existing in three persons – Father, Son, and Holy Spirit – is central to Christian theology and underscores Jesus' divine nature. The titles attributed to Jesus, such as Messiah (Christ), Son of God, Lord, and Savior, reflect his unique role in God's plan for salvation. The term 'Mashiach' (Messiah) holds particular significance, linking Jesus to Jewish messianic expectations. While some Jews accepted Jesus as the Messiah, others did not, leading to a divergence between Judaism and Christianity. The Aramaic concept of 'Memra,' the divine Word, is also associated with Jesus, emphasizing his role as the embodiment of God's communication to humanity. Furthermore, the concept of 'Sophia,' or divine wisdom, is sometimes used to describe Christ, portraying him as the incarnation of God's wisdom.

The cultural significance of Jesus Christ is immense and pervasive. He is considered the most influential figure in Western civilization, shaping art, literature, music, philosophy, and ethics for over two millennia. His teachings have inspired countless acts of charity, compassion, and social justice. Christianity, the religion founded on his life and teachings, is the world's largest religion, with over two billion adherents worldwide. The Christian calendar, which revolves around the birth, death, and resurrection of Jesus, has become the standard calendar in many parts of the world. The impact of Jesus extends beyond Christianity, as he is also recognized as a prophet in Islam and revered in other religious traditions. His story continues to resonate with people of all backgrounds, offering hope, inspiration, and a path to spiritual transformation. The influence of Jesus on Western art is undeniable, with countless depictions of his life, miracles, and crucifixion in paintings, sculptures, and other forms of art. These artistic representations have shaped the way people visualize and understand Jesus and his message. Similarly, Western literature is replete with references to Jesus and Christian themes, exploring the complexities of faith, redemption, and the human condition. From epic poems to modern novels, Jesus has served as a source of inspiration and a subject of intense scrutiny for writers throughout history. The ethical teachings of Jesus have had a profound impact on Western societies, influencing laws, social norms, and individual behavior. The emphasis on love, compassion, and forgiveness has shaped the development of social welfare systems, human rights movements, and ethical frameworks. The legacy of Jesus Christ continues to evolve as new generations grapple with his teachings and their relevance to contemporary issues. His message of love, hope, and redemption remains a powerful force in the world, inspiring individuals and communities to strive for a more just and compassionate society. The ongoing dialogue surrounding Jesus and his significance reflects the enduring power of his story and its capacity to challenge, inspire, and transform lives. The diverse interpretations of Jesus, ranging from traditional theological perspectives to modern critical analyses, demonstrate the complexity and richness of his legacy. Whether viewed as a divine savior, a moral teacher, or a historical figure, Jesus Christ continues to be a subject of fascination and a source of meaning for millions of people around the world. His story transcends religious boundaries, inviting reflection on the fundamental questions of human existence and the search for purpose and connection. The enduring appeal of Jesus lies in his message of hope, his call to love and compassion, and his promise of redemption and eternal life. These themes resonate with people of all backgrounds, offering a vision of a world transformed by love and a path to personal and social transformation. The continued study and interpretation of Jesus' life and teachings will undoubtedly shape the future of Christianity and continue to influence the course of human history. The ongoing efforts to understand Jesus in his historical context, to explore the diverse perspectives on his significance, and to apply his teachings to contemporary challenges will ensure that his legacy remains relevant and meaningful for generations to come. The story of Jesus Christ is a testament to the power of ideas, the enduring appeal of hope, and the transformative potential of love. It is a story that continues to be told, interpreted, and lived out in countless ways, shaping the world and inspiring individuals to strive for a better future.

summary: Jesus Christ, also known as Yeshua Ha-Mashiach, is the central figure in Christianity. Born of a virgin, he preached the kingdom of God, performed miracles, was crucified, and resurrected. He is symbolized by the Lamb, the Cross, and is considered the Messiah prophesied in Jewish scripture.

search.searchableText: jesus christ christian - jesus christ also known as jesus christ (yeshua ha-mashiach). symbolized by mashiach - jewish messianic expectations. also known as jesus christ (yeshua ha-mashiach). symbolized by mashiach - jewish messianic expectations. jesus christ jesus christ

worshipPractices.name: Prayer
worshipPractices.description: Prayer is a central practice, involving communication with God through Jesus. It can be individual or communal, formal or informal, expressing gratitude, seeking guidance, or offering supplication. Many Christians believe prayer can invoke divine intervention.

worshipPractices.name: Communion/Eucharist
worshipPractices.description: This ritual commemorates the Last Supper, where Jesus shared bread and wine with his disciples. It symbolizes partaking in the body and blood of Christ, representing his sacrifice and fostering spiritual unity with him and fellow believers. Different denominations have varying interpretations of its significance.

worshipPractices.name: Baptism
worshipPractices.description: Baptism is a rite of initiation into Christianity, symbolizing purification and new life in Christ. It typically involves immersion in water or sprinkling with water, signifying the washing away of sins and identification with Jesus's death and resurrection. It marks a commitment to follow Jesus' teachings.

festivals.name: Christmas
festivals.description: Celebrates the birth of Jesus. Observances include church services, gift-giving, family gatherings, and the decoration of Christmas trees. It's a time for reflection on the Incarnation and the message of peace and goodwill.

festivals.name: Easter
festivals.timing: Varies each year (Sunday following the first full moon after the vernal equinox)
festivals.description: Commemorates the resurrection of Jesus Christ from the dead. It is the most important Christian festival, celebrating the triumph over death and the promise of eternal life. Observances include church services, especially sunrise services, and festive meals.

festivals.name: Good Friday
festivals.description: Marks the day Jesus was crucified. Services are often somber, with readings from the Gospels recounting the Passion of Christ. It is a time for repentance and reflection on the sacrifice Jesus made for humanity.

cultural.modernLegacy: Jesus Christ remains a dominant figure in Western culture and beyond, inspiring countless works of art, music, and literature. His teachings on love, forgiveness, and compassion continue to influence ethical and moral frameworks, shaping social justice movements and individual acts of charity. The image of Jesus is pervasive in popular culture, often reinterpreted and reimagined in contemporary contexts.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Osiris was resurrected after being murdered, offering followers a path to eternal life, mirroring Jesus's resurrection and promise of salvation. Both figures are central to their respective religions and represent hope and redemption.

Dionysus, like Jesus, had a divine birth (though not virgin birth), experienced death and resurrection, and is associated with transformation and spiritual ecstasy. Both figures are associated with miracles and a strong following who partake in symbolic rituals.

Baldr's death and eventual return, while not a resurrection in the same way as Jesus, represent a period of darkness followed by a renewal, echoing the themes of sacrifice and hope found in the story of Jesus. Both figures are associated with purity and innocence, and their deaths bring about significant change.

associations.name: Lamb
associations.significance: Represents Jesus as the sacrificial offering for the sins of humanity.

associations.name: Crown of Thorns
associations.significance: Symbolizes Jesus' suffering and humiliation during the Passion.

associations.name: Water
associations.significance: Represents purification, baptism, and spiritual cleansing through faith in Jesus.

associations.name: White
associations.significance: Symbolizes purity, innocence, and the resurrected Christ's divine nature.

associations.name: Three
associations.significance: Represents the Holy Trinity: Father, Son (Jesus), and Holy Spirit.

associations.name: Morning Star
associations.significance: Symbolizes Jesus as the bringer of light, hope, and a new dawn for humanity.

associations.name: Love
associations.significance: Represents Jesus' core teaching of unconditional love and compassion for all.

companions.name: Mary Magdalene
companions.description: Mary Magdalene was a devoted follower of Jesus. She is described in the Gospels as being present at Jesus' crucifixion and resurrection.

companions.name: Peter (Simon Peter)
companions.description: Peter was one of the twelve apostles and a prominent leader in the early Christian church. He is considered the first Pope by the Catholic Church.

companions.name: John the Apostle
companions.description: John was one of the twelve apostles, traditionally believed to be the author of the Gospel of John. He is often depicted as one of Jesus' closest disciples.

companions.name: The Holy Spirit
companions.description: The Holy Spirit is the third person of the Trinity in Christian theology. It proceeds from the Father and the Son, and empowers and guides believers.

companions.name: Angels
companions.description: Angels are celestial beings who serve God and often act as messengers or protectors. They are often described in the Bible as ministering to Jesus or delivering divine messages related to him.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Miraculous Birth
extendedContent.content:
The narrative surrounding Jesus Christ's birth is foundational to Christian theology, emphasizing his divine nature and unique status. The Gospels of Matthew and Luke provide distinct, yet complementary, accounts of his conception and birth. Matthew focuses on Joseph's perspective, detailing his initial distress upon learning of Mary's pregnancy and the subsequent angelic reassurance that the child was conceived by the Holy Spirit (Matthew 1:18-25). Luke's account centers on Mary, portraying her as a willing participant in God's plan. The annunciation by Gabriel is a pivotal moment, marking the beginning of a new covenant between God and humanity.

Further emphasizing the divine nature, the birth narrative includes miraculous events and symbolic imagery. The star of Bethlehem, guiding the Magi (wise men) from the East, symbolizes Jesus's role as the king of the Jews and a light to the Gentiles (Matthew 2:1-12). The visit of the shepherds, representing the marginalized and lowly, underscores Jesus's mission to the poor and oppressed (Luke 2:8-20). These elements, combined with the virgin birth, solidify Jesus's status as both fully human and fully divine, a core tenet of Christian belief. The cultural context of these narratives is rooted in Jewish messianic expectations, with Jesus presented as the fulfillment of Old Testament prophecies. The use of symbolic language and miraculous occurrences served to reinforce the extraordinary nature of Jesus's birth and his destined role as the savior of humanity.

The infancy narratives also subtly address political anxieties of the time, as Herod's attempt to kill all male children in Bethlehem highlights the threat Jesus posed to earthly power (Matthew 2:16-18). The flight to Egypt, mirroring the Israelites' exodus, further connects Jesus's story to the Old Testament narrative of redemption and liberation. These details, while seemingly historical, function mythologically to establish Jesus's unique identity and his relationship to both Jewish tradition and the broader Roman world.

extendedContent.title: Ministry, Miracles, and Teachings
extendedContent.content:
Jesus's ministry, primarily documented in the Gospels, is characterized by acts of healing, exorcisms, and profound teachings. These miracles, understood as demonstrations of God's power and the arrival of the Kingdom of God, served to validate Jesus's claims of divinity and draw followers. Stories such as the healing of the paralytic (Mark 2:1-12), the raising of Lazarus from the dead (John 11:1-44), and the feeding of the five thousand (Matthew 14:13-21) are central to Christian belief, demonstrating Jesus's power over sickness, death, and the natural world.

Jesus's teachings, often delivered through parables and sermons, emphasize love, forgiveness, and compassion. The Sermon on the Mount (Matthew 5-7) is a cornerstone of Christian ethics, outlining principles such as turning the other cheek, loving one's enemies, and striving for spiritual perfection. Parables like the Good Samaritan (Luke 10:25-37) and the Prodigal Son (Luke 15:11-32) illustrate the importance of empathy, forgiveness, and the boundless love of God. These teachings challenged the established social norms and religious practices of the time, emphasizing a personal relationship with God and a commitment to social justice.

Furthermore, Jesus's ministry was not without controversy. His challenges to the authority of the religious leaders, particularly the Pharisees and Sadducees, led to increasing opposition and ultimately, his crucifixion. His cleansing of the Temple (Matthew 21:12-13) is a symbolic act of defiance against corruption and a declaration of a new era of worship. The conflict between Jesus and the religious establishment highlights the tension between tradition and reform, a recurring theme in religious history. The miracles and teachings, therefore, not only demonstrate Jesus's divine power but also his commitment to a radical message of love, forgiveness, and social transformation.

extendedContent.title: Passion, Death, and Resurrection
extendedContent.content:
The Passion narrative, encompassing Jesus's final days, crucifixion, and resurrection, is the climax of the Gospel accounts and the cornerstone of Christian theology. The Last Supper, where Jesus shared a final meal with his disciples, is commemorated as the institution of the Eucharist (communion), a ritual reenactment of Jesus's sacrifice. The betrayal by Judas Iscariot, often depicted as a consequence of greed and the influence of Satan, sets in motion the events leading to Jesus's arrest and trial.

Jesus's trial before Pontius Pilate, the Roman governor, highlights the political context of his execution. Pilate, though seemingly reluctant to condemn Jesus, ultimately yields to the pressure of the crowd, who demand his crucifixion. The crucifixion itself, a brutal and humiliating form of Roman execution, is presented as a sacrifice for the sins of humanity. Jesus's words from the cross, as recorded in the Gospels, reflect his suffering, his compassion, and his faith in God. The darkness that fell upon the land during the crucifixion and the tearing of the Temple veil are symbolic representations of the cosmic significance of Jesus's death.

The resurrection, occurring on the third day after his crucifixion, is the central miracle of Christianity. The empty tomb and the appearances of Jesus to his disciples are interpreted as proof of his victory over death and the promise of eternal life for believers. The resurrection transforms the meaning of Jesus's suffering and death, turning it from a tragic defeat into a triumphant victory. It is the foundation of Christian hope and the basis for the belief in the possibility of salvation and reconciliation with God. The resurrection is not simply a historical event but a powerful symbol of renewal, transformation, and the overcoming of evil.

extendedContent.title: Iconography and Representation
extendedContent.content:
The visual representation of Jesus Christ has evolved significantly throughout Christian history, reflecting changing theological emphases and cultural contexts. Early depictions of Jesus, found in the Roman catacombs, often portrayed him as the Good Shepherd, a youthful figure carrying a lamb, symbolizing his care for his followers (John 10:11-18). This image drew on existing Greco-Roman iconography, associating Jesus with themes of pastoral care and divine protection. As Christianity gained prominence, the imagery shifted to emphasize Jesus's imperial and divine status.

Byzantine art developed iconic representations of Jesus as Christ Pantocrator, the ruler of all. These images typically depict Jesus with a stern, majestic gaze, holding a book of scripture, and blessing the viewer. The use of gold leaf and stylized features conveyed a sense of otherworldly power and authority. In Western art, depictions of Jesus varied, often focusing on specific episodes from his life, such as the Nativity, the Crucifixion, and the Resurrection. Renaissance artists, influenced by classical ideals, portrayed Jesus with idealized human features, emphasizing his beauty and humanity. The use of perspective and realism brought a new level of emotional intensity to these representations.

Throughout history, certain symbols have become closely associated with Jesus, including the cross, the lamb, the fish (an acronym for “Jesus Christ, Son of God, Savior” in Greek), and the IHS monogram (a shortened form of Jesus's name in Greek). These symbols serve as visual reminders of Jesus's life, death, and resurrection, and are used in a variety of contexts, from liturgical art to personal devotional objects. The ongoing evolution of Jesus's iconography reflects the enduring power of his story and the diverse ways in which Christians have sought to understand and express their faith through visual representation.

extendedContent.title: Role in Cosmic Order and Salvation
extendedContent.content:
In Christian theology, Jesus Christ plays a central role in the cosmic order and the salvation of humanity. He is understood as the Son of God, the second person of the Trinity, co-eternal and co-equal with God the Father and the Holy Spirit. This Trinitarian doctrine, developed over centuries of theological reflection, affirms the unity of God while also recognizing the distinct persons within the Godhead. Jesus's role in creation is often emphasized, with some New Testament passages suggesting that all things were created through him and for him (Colossians 1:16).

Central to Jesus's role is his atoning sacrifice on the cross. Christian theology teaches that humanity, through the sin of Adam and Eve, is separated from God and subject to death and corruption. Jesus, by taking on human form and living a sinless life, offered himself as a perfect sacrifice to atone for the sins of the world. This concept of substitutionary atonement, while interpreted in various ways, is a core tenet of Christian belief. Through faith in Jesus Christ and acceptance of his sacrifice, individuals can be reconciled to God and receive forgiveness of sins.

Furthermore, Jesus is seen as the ultimate judge of humanity, who will return at the end of time to judge the living and the dead. The Book of Revelation depicts Jesus as the Lamb of God, the conquering king who will defeat evil and establish a new heaven and a new earth. This eschatological vision emphasizes the ultimate triumph of good over evil and the fulfillment of God's plan for creation. Jesus's role in cosmic order, therefore, extends from creation to judgment, encompassing the entire scope of human history and the destiny of the universe. His sacrifice offers salvation and reconciliation, and his eventual return promises justice and eternal life for those who believe.

shortDescription: Central figure in Christianity, believed to be the Son of God. Known for miracles, teachings of love and forgiveness, and eventual resurrection.

sources.author: Ehrman, Bart D.
sources.title: Misquoting Jesus: The Story Behind Who Changed the Bible and Why
sources.description: Examines textual variations in the New Testament manuscripts and their implications for understanding the historical Jesus.

sources.author: Meier, John P.
sources.title: A Marginal Jew: Rethinking the Historical Jesus
sources.description: A multi-volume work that rigorously analyzes the historical evidence for Jesus' life and ministry within its Jewish context.

sources.author: Crossan, John Dominic
sources.title: The Historical Jesus: The Life of a Mediterranean Jewish Peasant
sources.description: Reconstructs the life of Jesus as a peasant preacher and social revolutionary in Roman-occupied Galilee.

subtitle: The Son of God, Savior, Redeemer of humankind.
